title: Danish pastry whirl
subtitle: A laminated yeast dough rolled with cinnamon butter
excerpt: A Danish pastry whirl is a yeasted laminated dough rolled with a filling of cinnamon butter, then sliced into rounds and baked until the layers puff apart and the filling caramelises in the coils. The dough is made over two days: mix and chill, laminate and shape, proof and bake. The active work is short; most of it is resting time.
sourceNotes: Danish pastry (wienerbrød) is a laminated yeast dough of Austrian or German origin, popularised in Denmark and spread internationally through Danish emigrant bakers in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The technique and formula are well established in public-domain baking literature. The cinnamon whirl (kanelsnurre or similar) is one of the most common shapes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the shaped, unbaked whirls before the final proof. Proof from frozen overnight in the fridge, then bring to room temperature and bake.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be held in the fridge for 2 days after laminating before shaping and baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the laminated layers open quickly, so watch after 15 minutes

content.text: Danish pastry dough is a laminated yeast dough: a slightly sweet, enriched dough wrapped around a flat sheet of cold butter and given three single folds with rests between each. The lamination creates distinct layers that open up during baking; the yeast provides the slight tang and the extra lift you get from a Danish that you don't get from straight puff. The whirl shape is simply the dough rolled with cinnamon butter, sliced, and baked cut-side up.
Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer is worth checking before laminated pastry goes in; the temperature needs to be accurate.
Day one: mix and chill the dough

Mix the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add the milk and beaten egg and bring to a smooth, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes; just enough to develop some structure; do not over-knead, as the lamination will do further work. Shape into a rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate overnight or for at least 8 hours.
Day two: prepare the lamination butter

Place the cold lamination butter between two sheets of baking paper and beat it with a rolling pin into an even slab about 20 × 20 cm. The butter should be pliable but still cold; if it cracks badly, let it warm slightly for 5 minutes. Keep refrigerated until needed.

Roll the dough out to roughly 40 × 20 cm. Place the butter slab in the centre. Fold the two halves of dough over the butter, sealing the edges. Turn 90 degrees and roll gently to 50 × 20 cm. Give a single fold (one third over the centre, the other third on top). Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. You should see no butter streaks at the edge after this fold; if you do, the butter was too warm.
Second and third fold, with rests

Repeat the rolling and single folding twice more with a 30-minute rest in the fridge between each fold. After three folds the dough should be smooth and have a fine, laminated texture visible when you cut an edge. Rest for 30 minutes before shaping.

Mix the softened butter, brown sugar, and cinnamon into a smooth paste. Roll the dough to a rectangle of about 40 × 30 cm. Spread the cinnamon butter evenly over the surface. Roll tightly into a log from the long side. Slice into 12 rounds, about 3 cm thick. Place cut-side up on lined baking trays, spaced 5 cm apart. Cover and prove for 90 minutes until noticeably puffy.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush the whirls gently with beaten egg. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until deep golden brown and the cinnamon filling has caramelised in the coils. Watch from 15 minutes; the filling burns quickly. Cool on a wire rack. Eat warm; they are at their best within 2 hours of baking.
